question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to ensure that the Census Act (Northern Ireland) 1969 is reviewed and amended in a timely manner, so as to enable the same level of public access to census results as currently available in the Republic and the rest of the UK.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any Housing Executive Disabled Facilities Grant works deemed necessary to meet occupational therapist recommendations, including (i) the number and value, of applications completed and approved where costs exceed (a) £35,000; and (b) £70,000; (ii) within these categories, the number and value of applications currently not yet approved or completed, including the estimated costs, broken down by constituency, in each of the last five financial years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of post-mortems carried out in Adler Hay Hospital on children and babies from the North, in each year from 2018 to 2025.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of meeting requests he has received from representative groups or organisations from the LGBTQI+ sector, to discuss the Sexual Orientation Strategy; and (ii) the number of these requests he has accepted.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the last time he met with representative groups or organisations from the LGBTQI+ sector, to discuss the implementation of the Sexual Orientation Strategy.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any discussions he has had with his counterpart in the South on establishing an island-wide paediatric pathology service.
answer_text: As I outlined during the Assembly debate on 13 January 2026, I remain committed to doing everything possible to improve hospital consented paediatric pathology services, including exploring the potential development of an allisland service and the use of lessinvasive postmortem approaches.My conversations with my counterpart in the Republic of Ireland, while positive, have confirmed they do not currently have the capacity to undertake additional post-mortems from Northern Ireland. Their immediate priority is to build the necessary capacity that could support an all-island approach in the future through the training and retention of staff with appropriate specialist expertise. It is important to acknowledge that this is in the context of a global shortage of paediatric pathologists and progress will depend on factors beyond our direct control. My Department will continue to engage constructively with colleagues in the Republic of Ireland and in England to explore every possible option for improving these services for families at this most difficult time. However, it is not possible to be definitive on timescales at this stage as progress will depend on external workforce and system-wide factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her work to promote the real Living Wage.
answer_text: My Department continues to actively support the promotion of the Real Living Wage through its partnership with Advice NI, which has acted as the Living Wage Foundation’s accredited partner since June 2024. This partnership has delivered clear progress. The number of accredited Real Living Wage employers in the north has more than doubled during this period, increasing from 93 to 214. As a result, over 5,000 employees across these organisations have benefited from a direct wage uplift. I have engaged regularly with Living Wage NI to discuss how we can further strengthen this work and promote good quality jobs. As part of that engagement, I have written to Ministerial colleagues and to my Department’s partner bodies to encourage wider adoption of the Real Living Wage. I have also supported awareness-raising activity by contributing to Living Wage NI events, including speaking at a Real Living Wage campaign event during the Imagine Festival last month. This work reflects my commitment to supporting fair pay and tackling low pay across the economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to facilitate paid leave for Education Authority employees, who are also employed as Retained Fire Fighters, to enable them to attend mandatory training, as is the case for other services.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it does not currently provide paid leave for its employees, who are also retained firefighters, to attend training.  They do not have any plans to review this position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work his Department is undertaking to secure the land on the A2 Shore Road, Rostrevor, due to concerns about further landslides and to avoid the road operating with temporary traffic lights.
answer_text: The land referred to adjacent to the A2 shore road Rostrevor is under the stewardship of Forest Service. Land within Rostrevor Forest Nature Reserve/Rostrevor Wood SAC/ASSI is managed by NIEA in respect of its conservation value. In the interests of public safety, officials in NIEA have previously commissioned a specific tree safety survey of the area subject to a landslip during 2023.  Recommended works resulting from this have been completed. Geotechnical recommendations for the area included enabling natural regeneration to help increase resistance to erosion and officials are engaging with colleagues within the Department for Infrastructure in respect of any additional measures that may assist this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work his Department has completed in conjunction with Forest Service to secure the land adjacent to the Shore Road, Rostrevor, to avoid any further landslides.
answer_text: The land identified adjacent to the shore road is part of Rostrevor Forest and is under the stewardship of Forest Service. Rostrevor Forest Nature Reserve/Rostrevor Wood SAC/ASSI is managed by NIEA in respect of its conservation value.  Officials from both NIEA and Forest Service have previously worked in partnership to organise and complete tree safety works relating to public safety onsite, including adjacent to the A2 Shore Road.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for update on Go Succeed.
answer_text: Since its launch in November 2023, the councilled Go Succeed service has been a key element of our business support landscape. It had been majority funded through the Shared Prosperity Fund. However, the British Government’s decision to withdraw this funding now places the future of this vital service at serious risk.From the outset, the Shared Prosperity Fund did not fully replace the EU funding lost to the north as a result of Brexit. That shortfall has been compounded further by the introduction of the Local Growth Fund. Its Revenue–Capital split is entirely unsuited to local needs and has resulted in a 64% reduction in revenue available to projects previously supported under SPF.Both the Finance Minister and I have raised these issues directly with the British Government, but to date there has been little indication that they intend to address the impact of their decisions.I am continuing to work closely with councils and other stakeholders to consider options for the future of Go Succeed and to explore how we can collectively safeguard this important support for businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that the Senior Responsible Owner role is formally established within the public sector, to ensure accountability and good governance in major capital projects, to detail (i) the rational as to why why his Department has brought the Senior Responsible Owner role in house for the St Louis Grammar School, Kilkeel, capital build project; and (ii) any safeguards in place to maintain effective oversight.
answer_text: The Department’s Governance Framework for School Construction Projects is the key operating procedure and control framework for the delivery of all major capital projects.  This document sets out the overarching roles and governance on capital projects.  The St Louis Grammar School major capital works project is being progressed in accordance with the Governance FrameworkThe Framework details that the Senior Responsible Owner (SRO) will be a member of the Department of Education or Education Authority Major Capital Team, who will have considerable experience in delivering new build projects within the education sector.  Following the retirement of the previous SRO for the St Louis Grammar School major capital project the role of SRO was reassigned to the Director of Investment and Infrastructure, which is in line with the Department’s Governance Framework.  All safeguards documented in the Governance Framework are being adhered to in the management and oversight of the St Louis Grammar School project, which includes ensuring all relevant roles and responsibilities have been assigned, that all project team and project board meetings are taking place and that the Gateway Review process is followed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital build project for St. Louis Grammar School, Kilkeel.
answer_text: The design of the new school building is ongoing and a number of design workshops have taken place over the last number of weeks with school representatives.   It is anticipated that the final report for this stage of design will be submitted to the Department for review in February 2026.The planning application for the project was submitted to Newry Mourne and Down District Council on 26 September 2025. The current programme estimates construction of the new building to commence in the third quarter of 2027, however this will be dependent on receiving planning approval and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to 36069/22-27, to detail (i) the amount of seed funding allocated for primary care multi disciplinary team social work staff in GP practices, in each of the last ten years; and (ii) whether the funding is allocated (a) per whole time equivalent social worker in post; or (b) per head of patient in GP practices where a social worker is in situ.
answer_text: (i) The Department’s financial allocations for Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) seed funding commenced in the 2018/19 financial year. No seed funding was allocated prior to this period. The opening allocation for seed funding in each financial year since then are as follows:Financial YearFunding Allocated2018/19£100,000 2019/20£200,000 2020/21£350,0002021/22£297,5202022/23£314,3422023/24£364,9982024/25£375,4102025/26£455,994 (ii) The Regional Project Board agreed at a meeting in October 2022 that seed funding would be allocated at a rate of £0.50 per registered patient in GP Practices where at least one MDT role (Mental Health Practitioner, Social Worker or Physiotherapist) is in post. This replaced the previous arrangement under which seed funding was provided at a rate of £50,000 per Federation, based on an average Federation size of 100k population, with £2,500 per social worker. Formerly referred to as “Social Work Seed Funding”, the funding stream has since been renamed “MDT Programme Seed Funding” to reflect that it now encompasses all three MDT disciplines rather than being limited to Social Work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of funding allocated for primary care multi disciplinary team social work staff in GP practices, in each of the last ten years; and (ii) whether the funding is allocated (a) per whole time equivalent social worker in post; or (b) per head of patient in GP practices where a social worker is in situ.
answer_text: (i) The Department’s financial allocations for Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) social work staff in GP Practices commenced in the 2018/19 financial year. No funding for social work staff was allocated prior to this period. The opening allocation for social work staff in each financial year since then are as follows:Financial YearFunding Allocated2018/19*£298k2019/20*£2.02m2020/21£3.56m2021/22£5.24m2022/23£5.63m2023/24£6.42m2024/25£6.42m2025/26£7.55m*Please note that the data provided for 2018/19 and 2019/20 are estimates. Detailed allocations are only available from 2020/21 onwards. Prior to this, allocations were not broken down by profession.(ii) Funding for MDT social work staff is allocated per whole time equivalent social worker in post. Recruitment of social workers within GP practices from 2018/19 – 2024/25 was at a ratio of 1:5,000 registered patients. As of 2025/26, this ratio has been revised to 1:10,000 registered patients.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their assessment of the significance of the attendance of the First Minister at the Inauguration of President Catherine Connolly on 11 November 2025.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of unnotified inspections the NI Environment Agency carried out at the Re-Gen facilities in Carnbane Industrial Estate, Newry, in the last two years.
answer_text: The NIEA has undertook 25 unnotified inspections of Re-Gen Waste Ltd facilities in the Carnbane Industrial Estate in the last two years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of notified inspections the NI Environment Agency carried out at the Re-Gen facilities in Carnbane Industrial Estate, Newry, in the last two years.
answer_text: The NIEA has undertaken 12 notified inspections of Re-Gen Waste Ltd facilities in the Carnbane Industrial Estate in the last two years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department’s approach to inconsiderate pavement parking.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with various stakeholders to encourage parking compliance across the road network, encouraging all drivers to park responsibly and legally, and to consider the potential negative impact of their actions on other road, footway, and cycle lane users.However, whilst my Department has several powers under relevant legislation to prohibit and restrict parking, vehicles parked causing an obstruction, including on the pavement, where no parking restrictions are marked on the carriageway, are not enforceable by my Department and are a matter for the PSNI.My Department has taken a number of steps to address the ongoing issue of inconsiderate parking including introducing legislation to prohibit parking on footways alongside bus lanes, bus stops, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory ‘School Keep Clear’ markings, and encouraging parking compliance across the road network through the promotion of the ‘think before you park’ initiative.I recently met with the Inclusive Mobility Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) to understand further the issues that affect accessibility across our transport infrastructure here in the North, and how challenges like inconsiderate pavement parking can impact people’s everyday journeys. I welcomed these discussions, and I recognise the ongoing work IMTAC continue to do to address the transport issues that affect Deaf, disabled and older people within our community.I also recognise that more can be done in relation to inconsiderate pavement parking. Following the public consultation on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and the publication of the subsequent findings report, my officials have developed potential next steps for my consideration and I plan to issue a formal response imminently on the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the timeline for the appointment of language Commissioners and Director of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, launched on 5 March 2025.

To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the intervention by the Minister of Agricultural, Environment and Rural Affairs in stating that his Department is not in a position to support the Mourne Mountains Gateway Project being constructed in Kilbroney Forest Park.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools waiting on (i) an assessment; or (ii) training, for a school crossing person.
answer_text: (i)The Education Authority has advised that there are currently eight schools waiting on a School Crossing Patrol (SCP) site assessment.(ii) There are currently three schools waiting on SCP training which takes place when the candidate has cleared pre-employment checks.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline all public appointments made during the summer recess.
answer_text: Between 5 July and 31 August 2025 two new public appointments were made by The Executive Office. Claire Keatinge was appointed as Commissioner for Public Appointments for NI and took up the post on 22 August 2025 for a single term of five years. Joe McVeigh was appointed as the Commissioner for Victims and Survivors on 4 August 2025 and has taken up the post on 6 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on a review into the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: In February 2025, I approved a plan for the development of a new, evidence-based rural policy which would be undertaken through a co-design approach. This co-design process officially commenced in May and involves a wide range of stakeholders including all NICS departments, local councils, rural stakeholders and academia.  This important policy development work is being undertaken through three key strands – Supporting Rural Communities, Understanding Rural Communities and Championing Rural Communities.  Each strand is supported by a Working Group and these groups will work intensively throughout the summer to draft rural policy proposals to meet the objectives set for the Group. One of the objectives of the Championing Rural Communities Working Group is to develop proposals to improve the effectiveness of the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016. As the Working Groups are still undertaking their work, I am not in a position to provide members with any further update at this point in time.  It is my intention to consult on the draft proposals later this year before seeking the support from my Executive colleagues to launch a new rural policy for NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Spending Review 2025.
answer_text: The Spending Review provides the Executive with its funding envelope for the next three years for day-to-day spending, and four years for capital spending.While in accounting terms the Executive has additional Barnett funding of £3.1 billion in day-to-day funding over the three years, it must be viewed in the context of the many growing demands and the increased costs of delivering public services.  It is clear significant challenges remain for our public services.While it does not deliver everything we had hoped for, the Spending review does provide the Executive with funding certainty which will allow it to plan on a longer-term basis and creates the conditions for transformation. The agreement reached with the Treasury on the funding methodology has been important in delivering £603 million more funding across the Spending Review period than would have been the case under previous relative funding arrangements.  Importantly, it keeps the door open for further negotiations with Treasury to ensure that the Executive receives fair and sustainable funding that reflects our level of need.We also note the announcement of £50 million Financial Transactions Capital between 2026-27 and 2029-30 towards Casement Park, however this is not included in the figures until a profile is agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline why there is no specific post-primary provision for autism in the South Down Area.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that there are currently four post primary specialist provision classes supporting children and young people with social communication / autism within the South Down area.   Further information on provisions available within mainstream settings for all localities is available at:  Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools | SEND Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any conversations he has had with the Minister for Health in the South in relation to offering paediatric pathology services on an all-island basis.
answer_text: I am committed to finding a better resolution to the issue of local access for local perinatal and paediatric pathology.  Whilst my first preference would be the restoration of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland, such an outcome continues to remain incredibly challenging as the services are under considerable pressure not just here but across the world due to a global shortage of paediatric pathologists. I remain very keen to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to these services in the longer term that might mitigate the need for infant remains to be transported overseas.I had a number of conversations with Minister Donnelly about the possibility of future co-operation on this issue and I have raised this with the new Irish Health Minister Jennifer Carroll MacNeill TD.  These conversations have been very positive.  In the meantime, my priority is to ensure that bereaved parents continue to have access to high-quality post-mortem services that provides the medical answers as well as the care and support that families need.  Access has therefore been secured by a further extension of these arrangements with Alder Hey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will commit to working with colleagues in her Department's Southern Section to discuss progressing the unimplemented design for a road safety enhancement scheme for the B8 Hilltown Road, Newry, at the Derryleckagh Road and Crobane Road junctions.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.All requests for improvements to the road network, including road safety improvement schemes, are assessed in line with my Department’s current policies and guidance and all works are subject to prioritisation within the funding available.I understand that my Department had reviewed a potential scheme at Derryleckagh Road and Crobane Road junctions, however regrettably it was not economically viable to proceed with the project. In the interim, improvements were introduced to lane markings along with additional warning signs which have contributed to a reduction in injury collisions. I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the area to determine if any further improvements are appropriate.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  If driver attitudes towards speeding and careless driving do not change, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to rise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital build project for St. Louis Grammar School, Kilkeel.
answer_text: Education Advisory Branch are in the final stages of review of the updated Concept Design Report (RIBA Stage 2). Following approval of the Stage 2 design it is anticipated that the planning application for the new build school will be submitted to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in the second quarter of 2025.The current programme estimates construction to commence in the first quarter of 2027, however this will be dependent on receiving planning approval and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Regional Change Fund.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Chair and Board Members to the Victims and Survivors Service.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the progress of the Planning Improvement Programme.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils with a statement of special educational need in the South Down constituency are attending a special school or a Specialist Provision in Mainstream School.
answer_text: The table below shows the total number of pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs enrolled in special schools and approved specialist provision in mainstream classes (SPiM) located in the South Down constituency 2022/23.  School typeEnrolmentPrimary SPiM196Post Primary SPiM89Special154Total439Source: NI School Census

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the channels used by the Education Authority to liaise with bodies and organisations which make provision for individuals beyond the age of 16 who are diagnosed with special educational needs with a moderate learning difficulty in the South Down and Newry and Armagh constituencies, broken down by (i) the name of the providing body; and (ii) the name of any forum used for exchange of communication or liaison.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that its 14+ Transitions Service liaises with the following bodies and organisations which make provision for individuals beyond the age of 16 with a moderate learning difficulty (MLD) in the South Down and Newry and Armagh constituencies:Further Education CollegesSERC South Eastern Regional CollegeSRC Southern Regional CollegeEmployment and Skills ProgrammesClanryeRutledge (Armagh)People FirstVoluntary Organisations Barnardo’sMencapCedar FoundationYouth ActionThe Gathering Rooms (Armagh) / The Appleby Trust Day OpportunitiesSocial FarmingSouthern Health and Social Care Trust- Day Care and Day Opportunity Options The 14+ Transitions Service also works closely with the Northern Ireland Careers Service, the Health and Social Care Trusts and other statutory agencies, to support pathway planning for each young person leaving education at 16.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with a diagnosed special educational need with a moderate learning difficulty in each school and establishment in the South Down and Newry and Armagh constituencies, broken down by (i) Year 13; and (ii) Year 14.
answer_text: In total, there were only six pupils in Year 13 and Year 14 enrolled across six schools in the South Down and Newry & Armagh constituencies as of October 2022, who had a moderate learning difficulty recorded by their school as a Special Educational Need.  This is the most recent academic year for which Special Educational Needs information is available.Due to small numbers, the sensitive nature and potential identification of individuals, it is not possible to provide this information broken down by year group or by individual school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for the scope of any Education Authority post-16 provision in place, outside of traditional school settings, for individuals diagnosed with special educational needs with a moderate learning difficulty in the (i) South Down constituency; and (ii) Newry and Armagh constituency.
answer_text: I am committed to enhancing post-16 pathways for young people with special educational needs (SEN) across Northern Ireland. The department’s End to End Review of SEN includes a dedicated ‘transitions’ workstream focusing on individual supports and transition planning.  It is vital that there are support systems available to young people with SEN, ensuring they have access to tailored educational and vocational pathways to support successful transitions into adulthood with greater independence and employment prospects.The Education Authority (EA) Area Planning team has advised that it will seek to ensure pathways of provision post-16 are available for young people with SEN across the region, including the South Down, Newry and Armagh areas.My officials continue to work alongside officers from within the EA Transitions Support Team to strengthen the transition planning process, identifying improvements to create better ways of working and sharing of information with colleagues from the Departments for Economy, Communities and Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with a diagnosed special educational need with a moderate learning difficulty, in each (i) special school; (ii) post-primary learning support unit; (iii) autism class; and (iv) specialist provision in a mainstream setting in the South Down and Newry and Armagh constituencies, broken down by (a) Year 9; (b) Year 10; (c) Year 11; and (d) Year 12.
answer_text: (i) There were four special schools in the South Down and Newry and Armagh constituencies open in October 2022.  This is the most recent academic year for which Special Educational Needs information is available. There were seven pupils in year groups nine to 12 recorded with moderate learning difficulty recorded by their school as a Special Educational Need.  Due to small numbers, the sensitive nature and potential identification of individuals, it is not possible to provide this information broken down by year group or by individual school(ii) Learning support units are now known as specialist provision in mainstream (see (iii) and (iv)).(iii) There were less than five pupils enrolled in two post primary schools in the South Down and Newry and Armagh constituencies that are recorded with a moderate learning difficulty by their school and who attend a specialist provision in mainstream autism class. Due to small numbers, it is not possible to provide this information broken down by year group or by individual school.(iv) The table below shows the total number of pupils with a moderate learning difficulty attending approved specialist provision in mainstream classes by school.  Due to small numbers, it is not possible to provide this information broken down by year group.Number of year nine to year 12 pupils attending specialist provision in mainstream classes with a moderate learning difficulty 2022/23School NameConstituencyNumber of pupilsCity of Armagh High SchoolNewry and Armagh12Tandragee Junior HighNewry and Armagh9St Joseph's Boys' High School, NewryNewry and Armagh10St Paul's High School, BessbrookNewry and Armagh14St Joseph's High School, CrossmaglenNewry and Armagh6St Patrick's High School, KeadyNewry and Armagh*St Malachy's High School, CastlewellanSouth Down15Blackwater Integrated College, DownpatrickSouth Down7Kilkeel High SchoolSouth Down7St Mark's High School, WarrenpointSouth Down*Source: Annual School Census* Small number suppressed due to sensitive nature and potential identification of individual pupils

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the details of his pilot scheme for bilingual road signs in the Gaeltacht Quarter, Belfast.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the consultation on the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: Consultation opened on the 9th of September and finished on 4th November. During the consultation The Executive Office attended more than 70 events and engaged with over 1500 people to encourage them to share their views.Ministers have also attended a number of events to encourage people to get involved in the consultation process.Officials have held 17 workshops at which more than 500 people have attended. We received 1375 responses to the consultation.Alongside public consultation, we have been consulting on our Equality Impact Assessment, a Child Rights Assessment, and a Rural Needs Impact Assessment. Consultation on all three will close on 2nd December. 	All responses to this consultation matter, and Ministers are looking forward to considering consultation responses and finalising the Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her plans to bring forward proposals to update the rating policy.
To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the publication of the four social inclusion strategies.
answer_text: The development of robust, sustainable and effective Social Inclusion Strategies is a key priority for my Department and I have taken time to consider the work taken forward to date. 	I will bring a draft Anti-poverty Strategy to the Executive in early 2025 for consideration. Following Executive agreement of this paper the Strategy will be subject to public consultation prior to its final publication.		I will be writing to my Executive colleagues before the end of October to outline the next steps in developing the Disability Strategy and proposals for the Gender Equality and Sexual Orientation Strategies will follow later in the year. 		While the Department for Communities is leading on the development of the Strategies, final decisions on its content, publication and implementation will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when remedial works will be carried out to repair landslides at Shore Road, Rostrevor and Kilkeel Road, Hilltown, following the heavy rainfall and flooding last November.
To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the provision of courses in prisons for offenders.
To ask the Minister of Finance what progress has been made to develop legislation on marriage law reform.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans to address the lack of special educational needs provision for post-19 individuals in South Down.
answer_text: I acknowledge the need for provision of clear and appropriate pathways for all young people transitioning from education into adulthood and this is a key theme within the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN). My department jointly chairs a ‘Transitions Working Group’, with the Department of Health (DoH), which includes representation from the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Agency (PHA) and officials from the Departments for Communities (DfC) and Economy (DoE).  The work of this group will inform recommendations arising from the E2E Review of SEN on how relevant departments/bodies can improve pathways for all young people with SEN and disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his plans to reduce road deaths.
To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to increase Special Educational Needs provision in South Down to ensure children receive a place to start in September.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what financial support measures he intends to provide to farmers as a result of flooding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when his Department will publish the outcome of the Down Forestry Review.
answer_text: The planning review for Down forests is ongoing as part of the Department’s responsibilities for sustainable forest management across its estate. Stakeholder views received in response to the scoping stage of the forest planning process are expected to be published by the autumn, following which the Down forest planning review will be completed, and plans published online by December 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what opportunities exist for farmers in South Down following the award of Protected Geographic Indicator status for Irish grass-fed beef.
answer_text: Dear SinéadThe PGI for ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’ covers grass fed beef produced anywhere on the island of Ireland. In order to use the protected ‘Irish Grass Fed Beef’ name on their products, farmers need to be verified by a competent body that they are adhering to the requirements of the PGI’s technical specification. The verification mechanisms for NI producers are under development by the NI beef industry.Once such verification is possible, farmers in South Down who can meet these requirements will be able to market their beef within the EU with this protected food name and the PGI logo. While it is hard to estimate the benefits of this opportunity, a study carried out by the European Commission found that in 2017 the sales value of PGI meat products was on average 1.2 times the sales value for comparable standard products without a GI label.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how many applications there have been for Stalking Protection Orders since they were introduced.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the development of a strategy to address violence against women and girls.
answer_text: We are committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls as soon as possible. This is an Executive priority which has been developed through a successful co-design process involving people and organisations from right across government and society, including, crucially, those with lived experience. We, together with the Junior Ministers and officials, met with the Co-design group only a few weeks ago, reaffirming our commitment to this vital and necessary work. Preparatory work to enable effective delivery is already underway, and a draft two-year Implementation Plan is under development. The draft Framework is expected to be submitted for Executive consideration and approval in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people are on the social housing waiting list in the South Down constituency; (ii) how many of these people are considered homeless; and (iii) how his Department plans to work with the Housing Executive and other housing associations to deliver more social and affordable homes in South Down.
answer_text: A breakdown of the social housing waiting list and how many of these people are considered homeless, based on the criteria requested, is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. The ability to increase the supply of social housing is constrained by budget.  However, my Department continues to bring forward measures to maximise the Social Housing Development Programme through its tripartite arrangement with the Housing Executive and registered Housing Associations. Details of delivery in South Down is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.For operational matters, you should direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.Co-Ownership is my Department’s main partner for the delivery of intermediate housing. We have made a large commitment to Co-Ownership in recent years, providing £158 million in FTC funding to assist over 4,000 people into home ownership between 2020 and 2025, as well as £20.5m in total funding for the Rent to Own and Co-Own for Over 55s pilots.The Affordable Home Loans Fund provided £19m of FTC funding to 3 housing associations (Apex, Choice and Clanmil) to pilot an alternative model of shared ownership focused on new build. This pilot ended in 2022, and following an extensive review, officials are considering the next steps in the development of a replacement scheme.My Department is in the process of developing a grant funding programme to support the delivery of intermediate homes which are part of Mixed Tenure (mainly social) developments throughout Northern Ireland in areas where market failure can be demonstrated.  This funding will be available to Registered Housing Associations subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures have been put in place to allow adults diagnosed with ADHD to access and have their medication funded from the NHS.
answer_text: If a patient wishes to avail of HSC treatment, then the appropriate action is for the GP to refer the patient to a specialist provider within the HSC, for specialist care or with a view to shared care. Specialist providers within the HSC are the HSC Trust clinicians. Patients are referred to HSC specialists by their GP and cannot self-refer. This is particularly pertinent where there is a view to shared care, as shared care will be between the GP and the specialist. It would not be usual or expected for a GP to prescribe a specialist amber drug with the absence of an agreed shared care arrangement, as in doing so they would be taking on all governance for the safe prescribing and monitoring of a specialist medication which may fall outside their usual scope of practice. The additional monitoring for these specialist medications is considered beyond the scope of General Medical Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the cervical screening services review.
To ask the Minister of Education whether there is any additional assistance, apart from additional time, for children diagnosed with dyslexia who will sit the transfer test.
answer_text: As transfer test arrangements are taken forward independently through the School Examination Assessment Group (SEAG), my department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are being put in place to reduce the urgent red flag waiting times for women who are being referred to Craigavon Hospital Breast Clinic.
answer_text: The Department continues to work with all Trusts to reduce the current waiting time for breast assessments. Non-recurrent funding has been allocated to Trusts in 2023/24 to support additional waiting list initiative work, using both inhouse and independent sector capacity.  In addition, the Department continues to facilitate the equalisation and reduction of waiting times through the inter-Trust transfer of patients. With regard to the SHSCT service at Craigavon, recurrent investment was made to allow the appointment of a full time third consultant post. The additional consultant has been appointed with an agreed start date in June 2024. The Trust are exploring the recruitment of a locum consultant in the interim to ensure available capacity is maximised.Non-recurrent funding has also been made available to the Trust in 2023/24 to secure additional capacity from the Independent Sector.  Bringing our waiting lists to an acceptable level across a range of specialties, including breast services, is a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment and additional staffing. The scale at which I will be able to tackle the unacceptable waiting lists will be greatly influenced by the outcome of the current budgetary discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to commence the redevelopment of St. Mary's Primary School, Barr.
answer_text: The School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at St Mary’s Primary School, Barr, is currently paused at RIBA Stage 4 (Technical Design and Pre-Tender) and will be released to construction tender when capital funding is available for the project. I will continue to bid for additional capital funding and make the case for increased investment in the education estate to enable delivery of the many important projects across my Department’s capital programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many Autism-specific classroom units exist in the primary schools in the BT34 and BT35 postcode areas.
answer_text: There are currently nine autism-specific specialist provision classes in mainstream primary schools in the BT34 and BT35 postcode areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many vehicles travel on the Newcastle Road, Castlewellan each day.
answer_text: The Traffic and Travel Information is published annually on my Department’s website.  See link attached; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/traffic-and-travel-information-incorporating-annual-traffic-census-and-variations.The Castlewellan to Newcastle is Count Point Number 517 with the last record dated in 2015 as approximately 8570 vehicles per day (Traffic & travel information 2015, page 70).Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to list the legislation that is affected by the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill, within areas of the Department's responsibility.
answer_text: Work is underway to examine the scope and impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill to determine the legislation likely to be affected within areas of my Department’s responsibility. This is being carried out as part of a wider exercise being coordinated centrally by the Executive Office across NI Departments to assess the implications of the Bill. Until this exercise is completed it is not possible to fully identify a list of affected legislation or to be definitive about overall impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many vehicles travel on the Hilltown Road, Mayobridge each day.
answer_text: The Traffic and Travel Information is published annually on my Department’s website and can be viewed via the following link:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/traffic-and-travel-information-incorporating-annual-traffic-census-and-variations There is no permanent count undertaken for the stretch of road between Mayobridge and Hilltown although I am advised that a temporary count carried out in 2014, over the duration of one week, was approximately 6000 vehicles per day.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the work the Department is undertaking to assess the impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform Bill), within its areas of responsibility.
answer_text: Work is underway to examine the scope and impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill to determine the legislation likely to be affected within areas of my Department’s responsibility. This is being carried out as part of a wider exercise being coordinated centrally by the Executive Office across NI Departments to assess the implications of the Bill. Until this exercise is completed it is not possible to fully identify a list of affected legislation or to be definitive about overall impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when his Department will undertake an Equality Screening Procedure with the Student Loan Company to ensure that that there is no difference in approach for students who hold an Irish Passport as opposed to students who hold a British Passport.
answer_text: My Department has been exploring with the Irish Department of Foreign Affairs whether the Department of Foreign Affairs can facilitate the necessary information sharing and other operational matters required in relation to this issue. These discussions are ongoing.Whilst my Department has been seeking to progress this matter, given the key role of the Irish Department of Foreign Affairs, it is not within my Department’s gift to ensure that there is no difference in approach for students who hold an Irish Passport as opposed to students who hold a British Passport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what contingency plans her Department has in place should tenders in the Schools Enhancement Programme exceed the allocated budget.
answer_text: The construction market has been affected by sustained pressures in relation to the cost and availability of materials. While the rate of cost increases has now slowed, prices remain significantly higher than previously.An allowance is made within business cases for inflation and anticipated costs are regularly reviewed during the design stages. However, if it becomes apparent that the tender cost for a School Enhancement Programme project will exceed the business case approval due to inflationary increases, officials will prepare business case addendums to seek approval for increased costs. My Department has put specific arrangements in place for re-approval in those cases where the increase in cost is solely due to inflationary impact. School Enhancement Programme projects will continued to be delivered in line with available capital budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what legislation is planned for inclusion in her Department's legislative programme for the new Assembly mandate.
answer_text: An exercise has been carried out to identify legislative priorities across my Department and officials have developed options for a draft legislative programme for the new mandate for my consideration.While that process is ongoing, and in the absence of an Executive and a sitting Assembly, I am not yet in a position to confirm what form my legislative programme for the new Assembly mandate will take.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether she is aware that the Education Authority requires a signed GP form, provided at a cost, to demonstrate that eligible children with special educational needs are entitled to free school meals; (ii) for her assessment of this requirement; and (iii) and whether she will consider a cost free alternative.
answer_text: A pupil will be eligible for free school meals if they have a statement of special educational needs and is designated to require a special diet. In such cases, the EA has confirmed that a new internal procedure has now been put in place that will not require additional medical evidence to be provided by parents as part of the verification process for free school meal eligibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether court staff, police officers or prosecutors, involved in sex offence cases, receive any specialist trauma training.
answer_text: The training of police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).  I am committed to respecting the Chief Constable’s operational independence and the role of the NIPB. However, the PSNI have advised that they have delivered a number of internal training programmes that include elements of trauma. I suggest that you direct further questions about police officers to the Chief Constable.Whilst the Public Prosecution Service is independent of my Department, they inform me that trauma training sessions are offered to regional prosecutors and staff from the Serious Crime Unit and the Victim and Witness Care Unit. For further questions about this, you may wish to direct your question about prosecutors to the Director of Public Prosecutions.The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service offers a variety of support programmes aimed at protecting the health and well-being of staff, including the provision of confidential counselling services for staff who may have been adversely affected through their work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what plans she has to develop a new prison for female prisoners.
answer_text: Approval for the associated expenditure in the Outline Business Case 1 (OBC1) for the New Female Facility on the Hydebank Wood site was granted by the Department of Finance (DoF) on 25 May 2021. NIPS are now progressing delivery of the Outline Business Case 2 (OBC2). The OBC2 will provide detail of the full design and specification for the preferred option. It provides a basis for DoF approval to initiate the formal procurement process. The OBC2 is expected to be completed before the end of 2022.Delivery of the new Female Facility is dependent on the provision of capital funding, to be considered as part of the process of setting future year budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the recommendations from the Criminal Justice Inspection Review into the operation of Care and Supervision Units in the Prison Service.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service has developed an action plan to address the recommendations made within the Criminal Justice Inspectorate’s report into its review of the operation of Care and Supervision Units.The Northern Ireland Prison Service is responsible for 2 strategic recommendations and 8 operational recommendations, work is progressing at pace to deliver against all recommendations, with 3 of the operational recommendations fully implemented. The Director General briefed the Chief Inspector Criminal Justice Inspectorate Northern Ireland on progress to date when they met on 10 May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on legislation for new provisions to tackle organised crime.
answer_text: I am committed to playing my part to ensure that law enforcement partners have the widest range of tools possible to disrupt the illegal activities of organised crime and paramilitary groups and to pursue, prosecute and punish those involved in serious organised crime. Proposals for the introduction of new offences of directing and participating in organised crime were subject to a public consultation exercise from July 2020 to October 2020.  The Department is finalising the policy proposals in light of the responses to the consultation.  The aim is to bring forward a set of legislative proposals to reinforce the response to serious organised crime in this jurisdiction in support of the legal powers already in place.  This work is included as a key action in the Northern Ireland Organised Crime Strategy 2021-2024. An exercise is currently underway to identify legislative priorities across my Department and officials are in the process of developing options for a draft legislative programme for the new mandate for my consideration.  While that process is ongoing, and in the absence of an Executive and a sitting Assembly, I am not in a position to offer any commitments in relation to legislative developments within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline a timeframe for the introduction of a Hate Crime Bill upon restoration of the Executive.
answer_text: Addressing hate crime continues to be a priority for me.  Work is ongoing to develop policies across a range of technical and complex areas, aimed at improving the effectiveness of the current hate crime legislation following Judge Marrinan’s Review of Hate Crime Legislation in Northern Ireland.  The Phase One consultation, which closed end March 2022, covered a range of proposals including a new statutory aggravation model; sectarian offending in the context of hate crime law; special measures for victims of hate crime; prohibition of cross examination by the alleged perpetrator; elements of stirring up hatred and a call for views on adding misogyny to hate crime law.  My officials are currently analysing the responses and it is my intention to publish a summary of responses later this year. Policy development on the remaining proposals  covering stirring up offences; age and gender as protected characteristics; and a statutory duty for public authorities to remove hate expressions from public spaces, is due to begin this year with a second consultation to follow in early 2023. An exercise is currently underway to identify legislative priorities across my Department and officials are in the process of developing options for a draft legislative programme for the new mandate for my consideration.While that process is ongoing, and in the absence of an Executive and a sitting Assembly, all I can say at this moment is that a Hate Crime Bill is likely to be brought forward around the middle of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the Domestic Abuse Protection Orders and Notices pilot will commence.
answer_text: With the introduction of the domestic abuse offence completed the establishment of these Notices and Orders is now a priority work area for my officials.  Work has begun on developing the draft regulations that will provide the framework for the pilot.  A guidance document for practitioners, which will map out the process for the notices and orders, and explain how they will work in practice, is also being developed.  Initial discussions are also underway with criminal justice partners to map out IT and training requirements.  My officials have recently joined a working group led by the Home Office and Ministry of Justice, in order to learn from their experiences and help to inform policy development and operational preparations for Northern Ireland.In taking forward work in this area two pilots will be established to robustly test the model ahead of roll out across Northern Ireland.  Discussions are ongoing with criminal justice system partners to consider geographical locations for the two pilots, one urban and one rural.  Once suitable potential areas are identified, the proposed locations will be brought to a multi-agency Task and Finish Group for agreement.  It is hoped that this will take place over the summer, followed by a range of development and operational outworking, with a view to the pilots potentially being introduced towards the end of 2023.  This will be subject to the necessary budget, IT and operational issues being resolved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the recommendations from the Gillen Review Report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences, including how many recommendations have (i) been delivered; (ii) commenced but not yet been completed; and (iii) not yet been commenced.
answer_text: The Gillen Review made 253 recommendations for change. Good progress has been made towards implementing these, although further work is needed.More than half of the recommendations have now been implemented to some degree. 86 recommendations (34%) have been fully completed; 54 (21%) have been partially completed (this means that a pilot or temporary provision is in place and delivering outcomes, but that further work  will be required to establish mainstream services).In addition, scoping and/or preparation work is underway against a further 82 recommendations (33%).Work has yet to commence against 31 recommendations (12%). In almost all cases, these recommendations have been deferred due to the need for legislation that could not be delivered in the last Northern Ireland Assembly mandate.		I remain fully committed to achieving the transformational and cultural change envisaged by Sir John in his report.  However, continuing uncertainty around future year budgets may affect the scale and pace of delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the use of unregulated and unqualified psychological expert witnesses in child contact cases, in courts in the north of Ireland.
answer_text: I have read the recent article by the Guardian/Observer newspaper on the situation in England and note the concerns raised by the Association of Clinical Psychologists.   While I am not aware of any concerns having been raised regarding the Family Courts in Northern Ireland, my officials will continue to monitor the situation and liaise with colleagues in the Department of Health with responsibility for policy on Parental Alienation.Generally, the courts do not appoint experts but may be required to grant leave for the instruction of an expert by one or both parties.   On making the application, the qualifications of the expert are set out together with the draft letter of instruction.  As with all expert witnesses, they are obliged to complete an expert’s declaration in line with the Lady Chief Justice’s Practice Direction.  It would be a matter for the individual judge as to whether the evidence of an expert witness is accepted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of current legislation and rules around access to complainants' private information in sexual offence cases, following new rules published by the Attorney General's Office in England and Wales.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the guidance published by the Attorney General on 31 May 2022 relating access to complainants' private information in sexual offence cases.  In recent weeks my officials have been engaging with colleagues in the Home Office, PSNI and the PPS to explore what, if anything, further is needed in Northern Ireland.  As part of the implementation of the Gillen Recommendations, the PPS and PSNI have been working collaboratively to improve the disclosure process to reduce delay and increase confidence in the criminal justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to increase ambulance cover in South Down to improve waiting times for an ambulance to arrive.
answer_text: The pressures on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and the impact on waiting times in south Down, as across the entire region, are mainly due to a recognised shortfall in staff across the service, the continuing impact of COVID-related staff absence, and delayed handover times at Emergency Departments (EDs).  Pending the sustained investment which is required to address these issues in the longer term, a number of mitigating measures are in place which enable NIAS to provide cover regionally which is close to funded levels, however it is recognised that additional cover is difficult to secure in some areas and at certain times.NIAS seeks to maximise its operational cover through the use of staff working overtime including by offering enhanced rates for the most difficult to fill shifts, by redirecting staff from its non-emergency service to support the emergency service, and through the use of Independent Ambulance Service providers. As with all divisions within NIAS, ambulance cover in the south Down area is managed on a regional basis and the Emergency Ambulance Control room moves resources across the region to spread the level of cover.  The Control Room will dispatch the nearest available ambulance to the next most clinically urgent call waiting.The delayed handover of patients to EDs is a symptom of the wider pressures across the system.  In order to address these system pressures my Department has undertaken a Review of Urgent and Emergency Care, with a public consultation closing on 1 July 2022.In the meantime, work is ongoing between my Department, NIAS and the other Health and Social Care Trusts under the No More Silos Action Plan, which I published in October 2020, to continually try to reduce delays, enhance flows through the system, facilitate timely discharge of patients and coordinate better management of our ambulance resources.  EDs have established dedicated Ambulance Handover Zones in order to improve handover times and maximise operational cover within local areas, and NIAS has increased the number of Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers working at EDs to expedite the handover of patients and the turnaround of ambulances to respond to calls waiting.  In order to protect capacity to respond to the most urgent Category 1 calls, an arrangement has been put in place for ambulances waiting outside an ED to be immediately released to respond if there are no other ambulances available in the local area. In addition to the above measures, my Department has funded NIAS to undertake a significant recruitment and training programme over the past two years, during which time 80 new Paramedics, 130 new Advanced Ambulance Practitioners and 105 new Ambulance Care Attendants, have been trained.  A further 40 qualified staff have been recruited directly.In order to address the shortfall in staff in the longer term and enable NIAS to respond effectively to present and future demand, significant further investment is required.  My Depa
